metadata.description: Essential Chinese vocabulary for time expressions, dates, months, and temporal concepts

content.name: september
content.side_f: I'm going to China in September.
content.side_g: 九月 literally 'nine-month.' Months 1–12 are formed as number + 月 with no measure word. Time-when phrases like 九月 precede the verb: Subject + Time + Verb.

content.name: month
content.side_g: 月 means 'month' and is the building block for month names (一月–十二月). As a noun it usually takes 这个/那个 or a number+个 when counted (三个月 = 'three months'). Don't confuse 月 (month) with 月亮 (moon).

content.name: twelve
content.side_g: 十二 = 10+2. Note the apostrophe in shí'èr marks a syllable break before a vowel. 二 is used in counting and compound numbers; 两 is used before measure words (两个, not 二个).

content.name: number_day
content.side_a: (measure word for number in a series; day of the month)
content.side_f: What's the date today? (lit. today what-number?)
content.side_g: 号 is the colloquial measure word for day-of-month; 日 is its more formal/written equivalent (九月十二号 vs. 九月十二日). Always pairs with a specific number: 几号 asks the date.

content.name: week
content.side_g: 星期 literally 'star-period.' Synonyms 周 (zhōu) and 礼拜 (lǐbài) are interchangeable but differ in register: 星期 is neutral, 周 is written/formal, 礼拜 is colloquial.

content.name: thursday
content.side_g: Weekdays = 星期 + number (星期一 Mon … 星期六 Sat), but Sunday is 星期日/星期天, never 星期七. Day-of-week phrases, like dates, go before the verb without 在.

content.name: day
content.side_g: 天 means 'day' as a unit of time and, unlike most nouns, acts as its own measure word: 三天 ('three days'), 每天 ('every day'). Also appears in 今天/明天/昨天. Separate meaning 'sky/heaven.'

content.name: birthday
content.side_e: Nǐ de shēngrì shì jǐ hào?
content.side_f: What date is your birthday? (lit. your birthday is which number?)
content.side_g: 生日 = 生 ('born') + 日 ('day'). The copula 是 is required: X 的生日是…. To wish happy birthday: 生日快乐 (shēngrì kuàilè).

content.name: this_year
content.side_f: This year I am eighteen years old.
content.side_g: 今年 ('this year') is a time word and therefore does not take 在 or 的. Parallel forms: 去年 (last year), 明年 (next year). Age statements drop 是: 我十八岁, not 我是十八岁.

content.name: how_many
content.side_a: how many or much; to what extent; many
content.side_f: How old are you? (lit. you how big?)
content.side_g: 多 + adjective asks degree: 多大 (how old/big), 多高 (how tall), 多长 (how long). Used alone, 多 means 'many/much' (很多人 'many people'). Neutral tone 多少 asks 'how many' for larger or unknown numbers.

content.name: big_old
content.side_f: My older brother is quite grown-up.
content.side_g: 大 covers both physical size and age/seniority (大哥 'eldest brother,' 多大 'how old'). For children under ~10, ask 你几岁; for teens/adults, 你多大. Opposite: 小 (xiǎo).

content.name: eighteen
content.side_g: 十八 = 10+8, a regular compound number. For ages, always pair with 岁. In Chinese culture 十八岁 marks legal adulthood and is often celebrated.

content.name: years_old
content.side_g: 岁 is the measure word for years of age and must follow the number directly; no 是 or 年 is used. For babies under one year use 个月 ('months old'). Children often answer 几岁; adults 多大.

content.name: eat
content.side_g: 吃 is a transitive verb and almost always takes an object—use the dummy 饭 when no specific food is named (吃饭 = 'to have a meal'). Sentence-final 吧 softens to a suggestion.

content.name: meal_rice
content.side_f: I like to eat Chinese food.
content.side_g: 饭 can mean cooked rice specifically or 'meal' generally (早饭 breakfast, 午饭 lunch, 晚饭 dinner). 吃饭 is the default way to say 'eat' when no object is specified.

content.name: how_about
content.side_a: Is it O.K.? How is that? How does that sound?
content.side_f: Dinner tonight — how does that sound?
content.side_g: 怎么样 is a tag question soliciting opinion/agreement, always at the end of a suggestion or after describing something (…, 怎么样?). Also serves as a stand-alone 'How's it going?' or 'How is X?' question.

content.name: too_extremely
content.side_g: 太…了 is a fixed frame expressing a high degree, almost always positive ('too' as in 'wonderfully'). The final 了 is obligatory in this pattern. Without 了, 太 can sound like a complaint ('too expensive').

content.name: thank
content.side_g: 谢谢 is typically reduplicated; the second syllable is neutral tone. Can stand alone or take an object: 谢谢你/谢谢老师. Standard reply: 不客气 (bú kèqi) or 不谢.

content.name: like
content.side_f: I like to eat Chinese food.
content.side_g: 喜欢 takes either a noun (喜欢中国菜) or a verb phrase (喜欢吃). The second syllable 欢 is neutral tone. Stronger: 爱 (ài); weaker/polite: 还可以.

content.name: dishes
content.side_g: 菜 means 'prepared dish/cuisine' (中国菜, 做菜) and also 'vegetable' in context (买菜 'buy vegetables'). Measure word for a dish: 个 or 道 (一道菜).

content.name: or
content.side_e: Nǐ chī fàn háishi chī miàn?
content.side_f: Are you eating rice or noodles?
content.side_g: 还是 is used only in alternative questions ('A or B?'). For 'or' in statements ('rice or noodles are fine'), use 或者 (huòzhě). Don't add 吗 to a 还是 question.

content.name: but
content.side_e: Wǒ xǐhuan, kěshì tā bù xǐhuan.
content.side_f: I like it, but he doesn't.
content.side_g: 可是 and 但是 (dànshì) both mean 'but'; 可是 is slightly more colloquial, 但是 more neutral/written. Often paired with 虽然 (suīrán) at the start of the first clause.

content.name: we
content.side_g: 我们 is inclusive or exclusive depending on context; 咱们 (zánmen) is explicitly inclusive ('you and I') and mainly northern. Plural marker 们 attaches only to pronouns and human nouns.

content.name: oclock
content.side_a: o'clock (lit. dot, point, thus "points on the clock")
content.side_g: 点 = 'o'clock' and follows the hour number directly: 七点. The copula 是 is usually dropped in time statements. For '2 o'clock' use 两点, never 二点.

content.name: half
content.side_g: 半 follows 点 to mean 'half past' (七点半 = 7:30). Quarter past = 一刻 (yí kè); quarter to = 差一刻. 半 also works as 'half a' + measure word: 半个小时 ('half an hour').

content.name: evening
content.side_g: 晚上 covers roughly 6 p.m.–midnight; the second syllable is neutral tone. Time-of-day words (早上/上午/中午/下午/晚上) precede the clock time: 晚上七点, not 七点晚上.

content.name: see
content.side_g: 见 as a standalone verb means 'to meet/see (someone).' Common farewell frame: time/place + 见 (明天见, 晚上见, 学校见). Formal synonym 见面 takes no direct object (和他见面).

content.name: goodbye
content.side_g: 再见 literally 'see again.' In tone sandhi, 不见 shifts 不 to bú (before 4th tone). Casual alternatives: 拜拜 (báibái), 回头见 (huítóu jiàn).

content.name: england
content.side_f: He is British. (lit. he is England person)
content.side_g: 英国 = 英 (phonetic for 'Eng-') + 国 ('country'), a pattern used for many country names (美国, 法国, 德国). Nationality = country + 人: 英国人. Language = country + 语 or just 英语/英文.
